coredata.dc:title: Achieving room temperature phosphorescence in aqueous phase through rigidifying the triplet state and information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Qian
Gan, Lanlan
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence materials show promising prospect in various fields, howbeit, it is difficult to overcome their unstable triplet state originated from dissolved oxygen, resulting in the challengeable synthesis of water-resistance room temperature phosphorescence materials. Here, we raised a facile strategy to originally prepare one kind of carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence, and their phosphorescence with a high quantum yield of 23% is further activated in aqueous environment by forming the hydrogen bonds mediated complex between carbon dots and cyanuric acid (CA-CDs). Importantly, the bridge-like hydrogen bonds not only ensure the rigidify of the triplet state of CDs but also isolate oxygen, thus facilitating a small energy gap of 0.44 eV between the singlet and triplet states of CA-CDs, which provided the evidence for understanding the RTP appearing by another innovative way. Relying on their dual-emission behavior, the current RTP CA-CDs were successfully applied for the fingerprint recognition and advanced information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Aqueous phase
Carbon dots
Information encryption

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fingerprinting and information security by CA-CDs nanocomposite
Preparation and characterization of CDs and CA-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing ( cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis of fluorescent CDs and phosphorescent CA-CDs; (B) The mechanism for activating RTP in water.

(A) TEM image of CDs, inset: DLS of CDs; (B) FTIR spectra of CDs, CA and CA-CDs; (C) Raman spectra of CA-CDs with 0% and 50% water contents; (D) DSC-heating curves of the CA-CDs with 0% and 50% water contents; (E) XRD pattern of CDs; (F) XPS survey of CDs and CA-CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1 s (G), N 1 s (H), O 1 s (I) of CDs, and C 1 s (J), N 1 s (K), O 1 s (L) of CA-CDs.

Images of CA-CDs with (A) bright field; (B) ultraviolet channel; (C) blue channel; (D) green channel; (E) Images of CA-CDs powder with sunlight (a), UV on (b) or off (c) at ambient conditions; (F) Normalized UV absorption for CDs, FL and RTP spectra for CA-CDs powder; (G) FL and afterglow spectra for CA-CDs solution at 77 K excited by 355 nm; (H) RTP spectra of CA-CDs with diverse temperature; RTP lifetime decay of CA-CDs solution (I) and powder (J) at 298 K; (K) FL lifetime decay of CA-CDs solution; (L) Scheme of activating RTP emission for CA-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Images of CA-CDs powder with UV light of 365 nm on and off; (B) Images of CA-CDs stained fingerprints on the slide with daylight, UV light on and off, respectively; (C) Images of information encryption by CA-CDs/PVA and CDs/PVA composites.

(A) Schematic illustration of triple encryption information by CA-CDs/PVA, P-CDs, and CDs/PVA inks; Images of two dragon (B and E), peacock (C) and lotus (D) printed on paper by using CA-CDs/PVA ink with UV lamp on and off.

head.ce:title: Achieving room temperature phosphorescence in aqueous phase through rigidifying the triplet state and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we raised a facile strategy to prepare carbon dots with blue fluorescence, and their phosphorescence with a high quantum yield of 23.56% is further activated in aqueous environment by forming the hydrogen bonds mediated complex between carbon dots and cyanuric acid. Importantly, the bridge-like hydrogen bonds not only ensure the rigidify of the triplet state of CDs but also isolate oxygen, thus facilitating a small energy gap of 0.44 eV between the singlet and triplet states of CA-CDs. Relying on their dual-emission behavior, the current RTP CA-CDs were applied for the fingerprint recognition and advanced information encryption.

Room temperature phosphorescence materials show promising prospect in various fields, howbeit, it is difficult to overcome their unstable triplet state originated from dissolved oxygen, resulting in the challengeable synthesis of water-resistance room temperature phosphorescence materials. Here, we raised a facile strategy to originally prepare one kind of carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence, and their phosphorescence with a high quantum yield of 23% is further activated in aqueous environment by forming the hydrogen bonds mediated complex between carbon dots and cyanuric acid (CA-CDs). Importantly, the bridge-like hydrogen bonds not only ensure the rigidify of the triplet state of CDs but also isolate oxygen, thus facilitating a small energy gap of 0.44 eV between the singlet and triplet states of CA-CDs, which provided the evidence for understanding the RTP appearing by another innovative way. Relying on their dual-emission behavior, the current RTP CA-CDs were successfully applied for the fingerprint recognition and advanced information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials especially with room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) features have stimulated ever-increasing interest due to their unique singlet–triplet process, high signal-to-noise ratio and large stokes shift, which extremely extend their applications in multiple fields of bioimaging [1-3], light-emitting devices [4-6], sensing [7-9], information security and beyond [10-12]. Afterglow materials mainly consist of delayed fluorescence (DF) and phosphorescence (Phos) materials. To be specific, DF processes are associated with the radiative transitions originated from reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), while Phos emission is achieved through intersystem crossing from the excited triplet state to ground state [13]. Howbeit, the vibrational dissipation of molecules and atmospheric oxygen could easily consume the triplet excitons, thus effectively quenching the phosphorescence [14]. Recently, the common phosphorescent behaviors were observed from RTP materials as their anhydrous solid states [15], and obtaining RTP in aqueous media is still of great challenge. Meanwhile, the current RTP materials mainly include the complex with noble metals, inorganic materials involved with rare earth elements and pure organic compounds. Suffering from their sensitivity to oxygen, high-cost, complicated preparation procedures and cytotoxicity, RTP materials usually appeared as the crystalline/solid forms or only existed at low temperature [1-15]. Consequently, developing the convenient ways to synthesize metal-free RTP materials with resistance to the dissolved oxygen and aqueous environment are of vital importance and challengeable.

During the past few years, several researches focused on the preparation and application of room-temperature phosphorescent CDs (RTP-CDs), which exhibited the dual-emissions of fluorescence and phosphorescence [16]. Particularly, two ways were verified to successfully synthesize RTP-CDs, corresponding to element doping and matrix assistance. Basically, RTP characteristic benefits CDs more specific applications such as biological imaging, analytical sensing, information security, optoelectronic devices [17] and more. Element doping could achieve the effective spin–orbit coupling, thus promoting the intersystem crossing to accumulate the triplet excitons. Lin et al. [18] prepared the gram class RTP-CDs through the doping of nitrogen and phosphorus, which were employed for anti-counterfeiting. Feng et al. [10] synthesized the self-protective RTP-CDs by the doping of nitrogen and fluorine, and further investigated the mechanism of sensing pH. Matrix assistance was another way to activate the RTP of CDs, which could stabilize the triplet state and suppress the non-radiative transitions. For instance, Liu et al. achieved the multicolor afterglow of CDs through embedding them in a boric acid matrix [19]. Lin et al. took advantage of the covalent bond between the CDs and silicon dioxide to boost RTP in aqueous [20]. Zhou et al. incorporated N-doped CDs (NCDs) into composite matrices (the melting recrystallization urea and biuret) to achieve the phosphorescent phenomenon [21]. Besides, triplet energy splitting (ΔEST) and degree of oxidation are also critical for activating the RTP of materials [22].

Herein, we raised one type of phosphorescence complex by overcoming the quenching effect from water molecules and dissolved oxygen and explored the RTP mechanism in water (Fig. 1 ). Primarily, the blue-fluorescence CDs doped with nitrogen were originally prepared through a facile hydrothermal treatment. Meanwhile, the nanocomposite of CA-CDs formed once cyanuric acid was introduced, and the cyan RTP with a quantum yields of 23% was observed in aqueous solutions, which was resulted from the strong hydrogen bonds between CA, bound water and CDs. These robust hydrogen-bonds induced the CO bonds of CDs rigid, and further rigidifying the CA-CDs network. Importantly, the bridge like hydrogen-bonds stabilized the triplet states (Tn) of CDs and isolated the dissolved oxygen, which forbade the triplet-to-triplet transition from CDs to oxygen (3O2). Accordingly, the decreased nonradiative relaxation, barrier for oxygen and small energy gap (ΔEst = 0.44 eV) collaboratively contributed to their RTP emission of CA-CDs. Encouraged by their dual-emission properties of RTP and FL, we applied CA-CDs suspension not only for fingerprint imaging, but also for encrypting the key information.

Methotrexate (MTX, >99%) was purchased from Nanjing Duly Biotech Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China), and cyanuric acid, poly (vinyl alcohol) [PVA] were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Again, 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) was acquired from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Further, ultrapure water (UP) by an Aquapro AWL-0502-P water system was employed throughout all the experiments (18.25 MΩ.cm). Unless otherwise noted, all the commercial reagents were directly utilized with none further purification.

Initially, Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was employed to record the steady-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra (solution/powder). In particular, phosphorescence analyses were performed at 690 V PMT Voltage and 40 Hz Chopping speed with the Ex and Em slits of 20 nm; and fluorescence spectra were obtained at 690 V PMT Voltage with the Ex and Em slits of 5 nm. Likewise, a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) was utilized to obtain high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images at 200 kV. Meanwhile, we applied Zeta sizer nano dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, England) to collect the size distributions, and XPS analyses were achieved by using ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Then, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) was introduced to explore the functional groups of CDs and CA-CDs. In addition, a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England) was used to identify the fluorescence lifetime and absolute phosphorescence quantum yield of CA-CDs. Also, the phosphorescence lifetime of CA-CDs was acquired by HORIBA FL-3 spectrometer (Jobin Yvon) with the excitation of 355 nm at ambient environment. Again, a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was applied to examine the UV–vis absorption of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets. Eventually, the lyophilization by PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA) was conducted to obtain the powder of CDs and CA-CDs.

During the early, we synthesized CDs on the basis of the hydrothermal method. In particular, methotrexate (0.044 g) was introduced into ultrapure water (6 mL), and this mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and followed by heating at 180 °C for 3.5 h. Subsequently, the obtained deep-orange solution was subjected to the centrifugation at 9600 rpm for 20 min and filtered with 0.22 µm of microporous membrane to remove the agglomerated particles. Then, the supernatant was collected and followed by the dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for 8 h to remove the unreacted substance. Accordingly, the lyophilization was performed to obtain the brown powder of CDs.

For obtaining CA-CDs, the CDs solution (10 mg/mL, 1 mL) was first added to 10 mL of ultrapure water and mixed with 1 mL of NaOH solution (0.5 g/mL), and 1 g of cyanuric acid was introduced to this CDs solution with constant stirring for 12 h at 25 °C. Then, we performed 6 h of dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for CA-CDs crude products to remove the unreacted materials, and these products were therewith subjected to freeze-drying for obtaining the white powder of CA-CDs. Besides, the CA-CDs powder (0.8 g) was re-dispersed in ultrapure water (10 mL) towards the following use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fingerprinting and information security by CA-CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: During the process of recording fingerprints, the powder of CA-CDs (0.1 g) and 1 mL of UP water were first mixed uniformly, and the finger was dipped in a thin layer of CA-CD suspension. Then, the finger was gently placed on the surface of clean slide and moved away slowly, thus the white fingerprint forming on the slide.

For preparing CA-CDs/PVA composite towards data security, 15 g of PVA and 100 mL of UP water were introduced to a round-bottom flask with continuously stirring, and the mixture was incubated at 95 °C for 1 h, forming the transparent gel of PVA. By cooling to ambient temperature, 10 mL of PVA gel as well as 0.5 g of CA-CDs were mixed with stirring, and the corresponding product served as the RTP ink for painting. As preparing the fluorescent ink, 1 mL of CDs solution (10 mg/mL) was added to 10 mL of PVA gel with stirring. To obtain another kind of phosphorescent-CDs (P-CDs) ink, we prepared P-CDs according to our previous study, and their RTP could not exist in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and CA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, carbon dots with blue fluorescence were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal treatment of methotrexate (MTX) at 180 °C for 3.5 h. In particular, we utilized MTX as the carbon source and nitrogen source owing to its rich polar groups of COOH and NH2 as well as CN/CN bonds, which could readily form hydrogen bonds. Meanwhile, the bound water confined in a polyelectrolyte brush was benefit to enhance the hydrogen-bonded network [23], and CA with rich OH around was readily to connect with the bound water and CDs, and thus forming the stronger hydrogen bonds, which may stabilize the triplet state and activate the phosphorescence. Besides, we also employed two substance with the similar structure (cyanuric fluoride and melamine), and subjected to the same reaction with CDs in basic conditions, respectively. In contrast, cyanuric fluoride-CDs complex showed the fluorescence and weak phosphorescence, while melamine-CDs only exhibited faint fluorescent behavior (Fig. S1A and S1B, ESI†). Through CDs connecting with CA, CA-CDs nanocomposites not only exhibited blue fluorescence but also emitted cyan phosphorescence once UV lamp was shut down. Therefore, we speculated that CA-CDs possessed the enhanced hydrogen bonds with the bound water. Upon the optimized conditions, the suspension of CA-CDs emitted the cyan phosphorescence in water without deoxygenation while the optimal excitation/emission wavelength were 355/485 nm (Fig. S2A, ESI†). Compared with their excitation and emission at 385/460 nm for CDs (Fig. S2B, ESI†), CA-CDs showed the approximate fluorescence excited at 370 nm and emitted at 460 nm, indicating that the fluorescence emission scarcely varied after connecting with CA. Again, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 4%, while CA-CDs exhibited an elevated quantum yield as 19% (Fig. S2C, S2D and Table S1, ESI†). Additionally, the CA-CDs suspension behaved the excitation-dependent phosphoresce and fluorescence property (Fig. S3A and S3B, ESI†).

To identify the interactions between CDs and CA, the morphology and functional groups of CDs and CA-CDs were contrastively analyzed. Investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), CDs showed a spherical particle with the acceptable dispersity and average diameter of 11.8 nm (Fig. 2 A). Meanwhile, the DLS data exhibited the size distribution of CDs ranging from 10.6 to 15.7 nm (Fig. 2A, inset), agreeing with that of TEM. Besides, the zeta potentials of CDs and CA-CDs were measured to be −32.40 and −22.10 mV, indeed providing the evidence of CDs interacting with CA (Fig. S4A, ESI†). To explore whether CDs combined with CA through the chemical bonding or physical adsorption, their FTIR and XPS analyses of CDs and CA-CDs were further provided. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the broad peak of CDs ranging from 2500 to 3600 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH (3441 cm−1), NH (3383 cm−1) and CH (2980 cm−1). Also, the carbonyl groups emerged at 1672 cm−1 while an adjacent peak at 1597 cm−1 was assigned to CC/CN of carbon cores. Besides, two characteristic peaks centered at 1404 cm−1 and 1297 cm−1 indicated the existence of CN and CO bonds for CDs. Taken together, CDs were with the functional groups of OH, NH2, COOH, CONH2 on their surfaces, and possessed CC, CN, CN for their cores [9-11]. Compared with that of CDs, CA-CDs showed a stronger peak at 3448 cm−1, demonstrating the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and CA through bridged water [8,24,25]. Moreover, the stretching vibrations of CO for CDs shifted to 1707 cm−1 after combing with CA, and the stretching vibrations of CO for CA red-shifted from 1720 to 1743 cm−1 when connecting with CDs, which provided the evidence for the formation of CO···H bonds among CA, H2O and CDs [25]. Again, the enhancement and red-shift of CN indicated the increased N doping, and OH···NC bonds (3143 cm−1) formed in CA-CDs [24]. In addition, Raman spectra of CA-CDs with 0 % and 50 % water contents were recorded and analyzed (Fig. 2C). Briefly, Raman peaks of CA-CDs powder without water located at 555, 711 and 1701 cm−1, which were assigned to the out-of-plane bending of rings, in-plane bending and stretching vibration of CO [8], respectively. Accompanied by the increased content of water from 0% to 50%, the peak of 1701 cm−1 became stronger and the peak at 711 and 555 cm−1 showed the red shifts, suggesting that CO of CA-CDs interacted with water molecules through hydrogen bonds. To clarify the hydrogen-bonds in detail, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was also applied for CA-CDs with varied contents of water (Fig. 2D). More precisely, CA-CDs without water exhibited a broad endothermic transition ranging from 40 to 90 °C during the heating phase owing to the destroyed hydrogen-bonds between CA and CDs (Fig. S4B, ESI†) [10]. Meanwhile, the melting endotherms of CA-CDs were observed at −10.1 °C (50%) and 17.7 °C (0%), revealing the existence of freezing bound water and bulk water for CA-CDs [8,10]. These results confirm that the bridge-like hydrogen bonds and bound water play the critical role for connecting CDs and CA, thus producing the RTP emission.

Furthermore, we employed X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to clarify their chemical composition of CDs and CA-CDs, which regulated the phosphorescent behavior. Fundamentally, there emerged an intense peak at around 2θ of 26.6° for the XRD of CDs (Fig. 2E), demonstrating the layer-to-layer distance of 0.335 nm in graphite-like carbon cores [26]. Basically, CA-CDs as well as CDs consisted of three major elements of C, N and O. In comparison to CDs, the ratio of oxygen and nitrogen for CA-CDs increased around 10%, whereas their carbon element decreased from 65.38% to 44.35% (Fig. 2F and Table S2, ESI†), proving the successful connection of CA and CDs and increased oxidation-degree of CA-CDs. To be specific, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs was fitted to three peaks of CC/CC (284.7 eV), CO/CN (285.6 eV) and CO (288.2 eV), while CA-CDs exhibited the increasement of CO and COOH (Fig. 2G and J) [20,21,27]. Again, N 1 s bands for CDs included three kinds of N as pyridinic N at 398.8 eV, pyrollic N at 399.6 eV and graphitic N at 400.6 eV (Fig. 2H). During the process of CDs interacting with CA, pyrollic N transformed to graphitic N and a large amount of CN were introduced into CA-CDs (Fig. 2K). For O 1 s, the XPS spectra of CDs revealed the presence of CO (531.6 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), while COOH and bound water emerged in the spectra of CA-CDs (Fig. 2I and L) [8,28]. Overall, the increased amount of COOH and bound water facilitated the formation of hydrogen bonds among CDs, H2O and CA, thus rigidifying the structure, reducing the non-radiation relaxation and internal conversion (IC) to activate the phosphorescence [15]. Besides, the increased graphitic N clarified more doped-N in carbon core that extended the level of π-conjugation domain, thus accumulating the excited-state electrons and further promoting the RTP emission [8,9].

To investigate the microstructure and fluorescence performance of CA-CDs, their images in different fields were obtained by fluorescence microscopy. Under bright field, the fixed CA-CDs showed as slender needles and crossed with each other (Fig. 3 A). Simultaneously, CA-CDs respectively exhibited bright blue, green and red fluorescence when imaged at ultraviolet, blue and green channel excitation (Fig. 3B–D). Consistent with the above data, the fluorescence of CA-CDs behaved as excitation-dependent.

To further understand the luminescence mechanism, we explored the origin and properties for their afterglow. Initially, we obtained the white powder of CA-CDs by removing the bulk water to observe their luminescent behavior, and this powder displayed the blue fluorescence and cyan afterglow with UV lamp off (Fig. 3E). For defining the afterglow nature, the red-shift of the RTP emission peak compared with FL emission peak indicated that the solid-state CA-CDs emitted the RTP without shielding oxygen rather than delayed fluorescence (Fig. 3F). Meanwhile, the RTP of CA-CDs showed the broader stokes-shift compared with their fluorescence, and CA-CDs powder displayed RTP at 480–520 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 355 nm, which was consistent with that of CA-CDs suspension (Fig. 3F). Subsequently, CDs showed two characteristic UV–vis peaks at 238 nm and 335 nm, representing two types of electron transitions (Fig. 3F). Specifically, the stronger peak of 238 nm was ascribed to π-π* transitions of CC in aromatic sp2 domain, while the weaker one of 335 nm was mainly from n-π* of CO on surface defects [8,9,20,21]. Importantly, n-π* transition of CO of CDs was responsible for both RTP and FL emissions of CA-CDs solution due to the overlapping of this absorption peak and their excitation peaks (Fig. 3F).

To deeply investigate their RTP, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CA-CDs at 77 K were therewith recorded to estimate the energy gap (ΔEST) between the lowest T1 and S1. As revealed in Fig. 3G, the low-temperature fluorescent spectrum of CA-CDs solution exhibited a peak at 440 nm, and the corresponding afterglow spectrum showed a mixed band at 440–520 nm, which partially overlapped with the FL spectrum. These data indicated that both phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence (DF) existed at low temperature. Besides, ΔEST of CA-CDs was calculated to be 0.44 eV, demonstrating a relatively narrow energy gap between S1 and T1 [14,29]. In this regard, the small energy gap facilitated CA-CDs to exhibit the high efficiency of afterglow by promoting intersystem crossing (ISC) from S1 to T1. Additionally, the phosphorescence intensity of CA-CDs gradually decreased with the temperature from 318 to 368 K, showing that the thermally assisted process did not exist within this temperature range (Fig. 3H). Accompanied by the elevated temperature, the increase of vibrational dissipation resulted in the decreased triplet-excitons, and thus weakening the Phos intensity of CA-CDs. Therefore, the current afterglow was confirmed to be the phosphorescence at room temperature rather than thermal active delayed fluorescence.

To completely clarify the RTP behavior, their phosphorescence lifetime of CA-CDs suspension and powder were measured and be fitted by exponential function. For CA-CDs suspension, the decay spectrum was fitted by three components of lifetime as 526 ms (6.77%), 86 ms (30.42%) and 19 ms (62.81%) with an average of 289 ms (Fig. 3I). As shown in Fig. 3J, there existed two components of decay as 107 ms (82.13%) and 611 ms (17.87%) for CA-CDs powder (Table S3). This result demonstrated that the observed phosphorescence emission was mainly originated from two decay channels with an average lifetime of 386 ms, and aromatic carbonyls of CA-CDs appeared as varied bonding-state. Likewise, the fluorescence lifetime decay of CA-CDs solution was fitted with a short-lived component (26.89%) of 2.1 ns and a longer one (73.11%) of 24.8 ns (Fig. 3K), being separately ascribed to the recombination processes of their core and surface states [15]. Based on the larger proportion of 73.11%, their fluorescence of CA-CDs was mainly originated from the surface defects with the average lifetime was 24.11 ns (Fig. 3K and Table S4). In brief, these findings revealed the surface defects mainly referring to rigid aromatic CO were responsible for the RTP and FL dual-emissive property of CA-CDs.

Usually, oxygen (3O2) in aqueous solution could quench the phosphorescence of phosphor by the triplet to triplet transition. To understand the influence of oxygen to CA-CDs, we compared the phosphorescence intensity of CA-CDs with N2 and air (Fig. S5, ESI†), suggesting their resistance to dissolved oxygen. Also, the singlet oxygen sensor green reagent (SOSG) was introduced to provide the evidence for CA-CDs isolating dissolved oxygen. Importantly, the FL of CDs showed around 7.5-fold higher than that of the blank after illuminated for two minutes (Fig. S6A, ESI†), demonstrating that a large amount of singlet oxygen generated though the triplet transition from CDs to surrounding 3O2. In striking contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CA-CDs scarcely changed with or without mixing with SOSG (Fig. S6B, ESI†). These results suggested the excellent ability of CA-CDs for isolating the dissolved oxygen, thus further preventing the electron transfer between CA-CDs and 3O2.

Taken all the above results together, RTP triplet-emission mechanism was established to illuminate the cyan phosphorescence of CA-CDs solution (Fig. 3L). In particular, the CO electrons of CA-CDs are excited from ground state of S0 to excited state of Sn after absorbing the photons (λex = 355 nm), and these electrons of Sn return to S1 through internal conversion. Next, partial electrons of the lowest singlet excited state S1 fall back to S0 (S1 → S0) by radiative transitions, producing blue fluorescence emission. Simultaneously, most of the other S1 electrons transfer to T1 via efficient intersystem crossing (ISC), and then return to S0 (S1···T1 → S0), thus achieving cyan RTP for rigid CA-CDs.

Through systematically analyzing the structure of CA-CDs, we here comprehensively explored the origin of their unique water-resistance phosphorescence: (i) There existed high content of O element for CA-CDs, which promoted to form a large amount of CO on their surfaces of CA-CDs. In this regard, the n-π* transitions (S0 → Sn) of these carbonyls could facilitate the intersystem crossing (S1···T1) to accumulate the triplet-state excitons as depicted in Fig. 3L [30,31]. (ii) During the stirring reaction, the hydrogen-bond net like bridges formed among the CDs, bound water and CA molecules (Fig. 3L), and rigidifying CO and the whole system by suppressing the intramolecular vibration of CDs [15]. (iii) The CA layer served as a protective screen to isolate the dissolved oxygen in bulk water, thus greatly avoiding the quenching effect from O2 [8,30]. Eventually, the cyan phosphorescence of CA-CDs with a quantum yield of 23% was successfully activated at room temperature in aqueous dispersion (Fig. S7, ESI†).

Basically, the obtained CA-CDs powder through lyophilization showed the bright blue fluorescence with UV light of 365 nm, while the cyan phosphorescence with UV off lasted for 5 s at room temperature by naked eyes (Fig. 4 A). Encouraged by the water-resistant RTP and FL performance of CA-CDs, we thus explored their applications for fingerprint recognition and information security.

Since the powder of CA-CDs could overcome the aggregation-induced quenching, we first achieved the rapid fingerprint imaging. A white fingerprint stained by CA-CDs was clearly presented with sunlight, and the blue-fluorescence and cyan-phosphorescence fingerprint were observed with UV lamp on and off, respectively (Fig. 4B). Additionally, CDs/PVA with FL only as well as CA-CDs/PVA with the RTP and FL dual signals were employed together for information encryption. The capital letters of “EAST” and “SOUTH” were separately stained by CA-CDs/PVA and CDs/PVA, thus mixing to be “ESAOUSTH” with UV on, and the data was decrypted as “EAST” after switching off UV (Fig. 4C). Similarly, the number of “8888” was recognized with UV of 365 nm, while the decryption information of “2019” was observed with UV off.

Encouraged by their superior RTP property of CA-CDs with wet or dry conditions, we therewith proposed a strategy of triple information protection. Simultaneously, we introduced another kind of CDs ink with phosphorescence (P-CDs), which were prepared in our previous study, and this blue-fluorescence P-CDs only exhibited green RTP under dry conditions. In brief, we employed CA-CDs/PVA, P-CDs and CDs/PVA inks to write the “3698989” on the paper (Fig. 5 A). To protect the right data, the interference information of “1573164” was first handwritten by CA-CDs/PVA ink, and then P-CDs and CDs/PVA inks were utilized to provide the second interference information of “3698989”. Consequently, the wrong information of “3698989” and “1573164” was visible with FL and Wet-RTP modes, and the cryptic data was protected very well as H2O existing in the air. To obtain the real data, the paper was dried at 80 °C for 2 h, and the decrypted data of “7639469” was observed with Dry-RTP mode. These results demonstrate that CA-CDs show the promising prospect towards the advanced information security. In addition, two dragons (Fig. 5B and 5E), peacock (Fig. 5C) and lotus (Fig. 5D) were painted with CA-CDs/PVA ink, and showed the blue fluorescence with UV on and bright cyan phosphorescence when UV was off, further suggesting the favorable water-resistant RTP property of CA-CDs.

In summary, the CD-based phosphorescent material was synthesized by CDs interacting with CA molecules through the hydrogen-bond network, which activate CA-CDs to emit the cyan room temperature phosphorescence in aqueous media. After connecting with CA, CA-CDs were functioned with more CO on their surface and pyridine nitrogen in the carbon core, and these structure variations were important for activating RTP. To be specific, CO groups on the surfaces of CDs, CA and water molecules formed the bridged hydrogen bonds, and this hydrogen network effectively enhanced the rigidity of the whole system. Then, the rigid system suppressed the intramolecular vibration of CDs and served as the barrier for dissolved oxygen, and further promoted the intersystem crossing with a small energy gap of 0.44 eV, thus producing the water-resistant RTP. More importantly, CA-CDs with both RTP and FL signals were applied for advanced information encryption. The proposed study raises a way of preparing the water-resistant phosphorescent material.

Qian Su: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Lanlan Gan: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.150726.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
